artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Martinez <daniel.martinez90@gmail.com>
To: Elena Martinez <[redacted]>
Date: 2026-01-13 08:17 AM
Subject: I scared myself last night

Hey Lena,

I’m writing this from the couch with cold coffee because I didn’t really sleep. I need to tell you something I’m not proud of.

Last night (Monday 1/12), around 11:45 p.m., I was here in the apartment on Hawthorne, just staring at the TV on mute, and I completely spiraled. I ended up emailing Kevin from my personal Gmail and told him I was “seriously thinking about ending my life” and that if I didn’t show up for work today to please tell the team I was sorry. I hit send and immediately felt sick. I can’t believe I put that on my supervisor, but that’s honestly where my head was.

I’m supposed to be logging into Cascade right now, answering tickets and pretending everything’s fine, but I’m shaking and just keep rereading what I wrote him. It feels like this giant red flag I can’t ignore anymore. Between the written warning he gave me back on 12/22 about my missed tickets and the whole “30 days to fix things or risk a PIP/termination” talk in his office, my brain just keeps looping that losing this job will prove I’m a failure.

I know you’ve got the kids and work and a million things, but can we talk later tonight? I’m scared of being alone with my thoughts right now. I don’t want to freak Mom and Dad out yet, but I also don’t trust myself as much as I did even a week ago.

Love you,
Daniel

Thread: Daniel & Mamá (Rosa)
Date: 2026-01-11, evening

Daniel: Mamá, can you talk for a minute?

Rosa: Estoy con tu papá viendo la novela pero dime, mijo. ¿Todo bien?

Daniel: Not really. Mark taped a 72-hour notice to my door on Saturday (1/10). I’m about $1,450 behind on rent here at 1847 SE Hawthorne, like six weeks. I just froze.

Rosa: ¿Cómo que seis semanas, Daniel? Why didn’t you tell us?

Daniel: I was embarrassed. I spent the whole weekend googling “painless ways to die” instead of calling him to work out a payment plan. I know how that sounds. I just felt completely stuck.

Rosa: No quiero que termines como tu primo Luis, ¿me oyes? Desde la Navidad te lo dije en la cocina, “por favor no termines como tu primo Luis,” después de lo que pasó en 2019 cuando no le dieron cama en el hospital. Me asusta pensar que estás pensando así.

Daniel: I keep hearing you say that. I’m scared too. I don’t want to end up like Luis, Mamá. But between cutting my hours at Cascade last year because of the panic on calls, the Visa being maxed from all the $40 copays, and now this rent thing, it just feels like everything is collapsing.

Rosa: Escúchame. Tu papá y yo ya estamos ayudando con los $650 cada mes para el apartamento, y vamos a ver qué más podemos hacer. Pero si sigues pensando en morirte, tienes que hablar con la doctora, con alguien. No te quedes callado.

Daniel: Okay. I’ll call Laura or Dr. Chen tomorrow. I’m sorry, Mamá.

Rosa: No me pidas perdón, mijo. Nada de eso vale más que tu vida.

metadata.title: Text thread with Mamá about rent notice

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Martinez <daniel.martinez90@gmail.com>
To: Emily Chen <[clinic address redacted]>
Date: 2026-01-09 09:02 AM
Subject: Update after yesterday with Laura

Hi Dr. Chen,

I wanted to give you a quick update after my session yesterday (1/8) with Laura Henderson at Riverside Behavioral Health.

I finally told her that in the past week I’ve already driven out to the I-84 overpass twice, both times around 1:00 a.m., basically to rehearse how I’d drive off. I parked nearby, watched the traffic, and tried to time in my head how it would work. Saying it out loud in her office made it feel way more real and scary.

Laura was really concerned and said if things got any worse she might have to initiate an involuntary hold. She asked me to reach out to you and to keep all sharp objects and meds locked down. I’m still on the 150 mg sertraline you started after Dr. Patel’s inpatient stay last year, but it feels like it’s not touching this level of hopelessness anymore.

I keep thinking about how, during that March 4–22, 2024 partial hospitalization at Providence Portland Medical Center after the overdose on my mom’s clonazepam, the structure and daily check-ins actually helped stabilize me. Coming home to my apartment alone afterward, with only 1–2 short follow-up visits approved each month because of the way my in-network benefits were counted, has felt really flimsy by comparison.

I know your schedule is packed, but if there’s any way to get a sooner appointment to talk about meds and options (including inpatient again if needed), I’d appreciate it. I don’t want this to get to the point where I’m back in the ED after another attempt.

Thank you,
Daniel Martinez
DOB: 1990-08-14
Phone: (503) 427-9812

Personal journal – work/insurance mess
Date: 2024-06-18
Location: Apartment – 1847 SE Hawthorne, Apt 3B

I need to get this all in one place because my brain feels scrambled.

Insurance: Aetna through Cascade Insight Systems. $40 copay per session, 20-visit annual cap for outpatient therapy. I hit the cap by 5/28/24 because I was seeing Laura weekly after the March partial at Providence and then twice a week in April when the intrusive thoughts about jumping off the Hawthorne Bridge on my way home from the office got really bad.

On top of that, my parents are already paying $650 a month to help me stay in this apartment since I cut back hours at Cascade in Feb 2024 due to panic attacks on client calls. I’ve been throwing those $40 copays for March and April on the Visa. Now the card is $3,200 over the limit and I’ve had two collection calls already this June about the missed payments. I hate that Mom and Dad are sacrificing so much and I’m still drowning.

In-network options: the Kaiser Interstate CBT group from Sept–Nov 2023 was a disaster. I had panic attacks twice in the group room and once in the parking garage after that guy described his suicide attempt in graphic detail. Dr. Chen even wrote on 11/17/23 that more group treatment there is clinically contraindicated for me.

I keep asking myself how I’m supposed to “use my supports” when the system literally cuts me off at 20 visits and funnels me back toward a group setting that makes me worse. No wonder the darker thoughts keep creeping back in on those late drives over the river.

metadata.title: Journal – therapy cap + money stress
metadata.location: 1847 SE Hawthorne Blvd, Apt 3B, Portland, OR 97214

artifacts.content:
Thread: Daniel & Miguel
Date: 2024-03-30, 9:14 PM

Daniel: Hey man, you free to talk for a bit? Rough psych appointment yesterday.

Miguel: Yeah, what happened? Thought you finally got in with someone in-network.

Daniel: Sort of. The only in-network psychiatrist within like 30 miles who could see me before December 2024 was at Kaiser East Interstate. I had the intake on 3/29.

Daniel: He spent less than 10 minutes with me, mispronounced “Martinez” like three times, and then literally said, “You’re a Latino guy in tech, maybe just cut back on the weed,” instead of talking about the sertraline Dr. Patel started after the Providence stay.

Miguel: Wow. That’s messed up.

Daniel: Yeah. I told him I’d just come out of that March 4–22 partial hospitalization at Providence Portland Medical Center after overdosing on Mom’s clonazepam, and that Laura and Dr. Patel had worked out a safety plan. He barely looked up from the computer.

Miguel: So what now?

Daniel: I don’t feel safe staying in that system. Meanwhile, my Aetna plan through Cascade only covers 1–2 short follow-ups a month now because they counted that partial against my outpatient visits. Mom and Dad are already helping with $650/month for rent on Hawthorne since I cut back my hours. I’ve been putting the $40 copays on a Visa that’s basically maxed.

Miguel: This is so backwards. You almost die, and their solution is “less care, more bills.”

Daniel: Exactly. I’m going to keep seeing Laura even if it means out-of-network stuff and more debt. Better that than another night like the one that landed me in Providence.

Miguel: Call me anytime, dude. Seriously.

Daniel: Thanks. I mean it.

metadata.title: Text thread with Miguel about Kaiser psych

artifacts.content:
From: Daniel Martinez <daniel.martinez90@gmail.com>
To: Laura Henderson <[clinic address redacted]>
Date: 2025-12-27 07:56 PM
Subject: Family stuff that came up over Christmas

Hi Laura,

I hope you got some rest over the holiday. I wanted to write down a few things while they’re still fresh, before our next session at Providence Behavioral Health Associates.

On Christmas Day at my parents’ place in Gresham, my mom pulled me into the kitchen while everyone else was in the living room. She grabbed my hands and said, “Please don’t end up like your cousin Luis,” and reminded me how he died by suicide in 2019 after being sent home because there were no inpatient beds. Ever since, she’s been terrified any time one of us seems “off.”

I’ve been thinking about that conversation nonstop, especially with how rough work has been. On 12/22, Kevin called me into his office at Cascade and gave me a formal written warning about my missed tickets and slow response times. He said if my performance doesn’t improve within 30 days I could end up on a performance improvement plan or even terminated. My brain immediately went to, “If I lose this job, it proves I’m a failure and maybe I should just go through with my plan.”

It all feels tangled together: Mom’s fear about me ending up like Luis, the warning hanging over my head, and the fact that my insurance keeps limiting care. I’m scared that if there’s ever a delay getting a bed again, I’ll get sent home like he did.

I’d like to talk about how to build a safety plan that doesn’t depend entirely on a system that already failed someone in our family.

Best,
Daniel

metadata.subject: Family stuff that came up over Christmas

artifacts.content:
Event: Crisis line call + ED visit
Date: Thursday, 2026-01-15
Time: 9:30 AM – 4:30 PM
Location: Providence Portland Medical Center – 4805 NE Glisan St, Portland, OR 97213 (Inpatient Behavioral Health Unit)
Attendees: Daniel Martinez, ED behavioral health team, Michael R. Patel, MD

Notes:

Morning at apartment on SE Hawthorne. Woke up after maybe 3 hours of broken sleep. Couldn’t answer any Cascade Insight Systems tickets. Sat in front of laptop from about 7:00–9:00 a.m. doing nothing but thinking about the I-84 overpass and the two rehearsal drives around 1 a.m. last week.

9:30 a.m.: Called local crisis line from my couch. Told the worker I’ve been having daily suicidal thoughts with intent, a detailed plan involving driving off the overpass late at night when traffic is light, and that three days ago I actually drove there, parked, and rehearsed it. Also mentioned the 72-hour notice from Mark for being ~$1,450 behind on rent, the email I sent Kevin on 1/12 saying I was “seriously thinking about ending my life,” and how I don’t feel safe alone here.

They used the Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale and said I’m high risk. Strongly urged me to go to the ED immediately and not drive myself if possible.

11:00 a.m.: Arrived at Providence Portland Medical Center ED. Evaluated by psychiatry, including Dr. Michael R. Patel. Reviewed history: March 4–22, 2024 partial after overdose on Mom’s clonazepam; sertraline titrated up to 150 mg with limited benefit; exhausted in-network outpatient visits (20-cap, $40 copays, collections calls).

Afternoon: Admitted to Inpatient Behavioral Health Unit for 24-hour supervision and medication adjustment. Plan to coordinate with Laura Henderson and Dr. Emily Chen and look at partial hospitalization or IOP step-down after discharge.

metadata.title: Crisis line + ED admission – Providence Portland
metadata.location: Providence Portland Medical Center – Inpatient Behavioral Health Unit
